tokens: Then we've got to turn our H2O off.
Oh, we've got to turn our...Let's turn our water off first, before you lock it (meaning the hatch).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Can you get your own water?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Before you move any more, let me get over here out of the way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And pay attention here, 17, when you come on, we'd like you to leave
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Turn some more, I can almost reach it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, that ought to do it.
(Pause) It's Auto and I've got the lock on it.
Now, Bob, say again which Reg A you want left.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Reg A left closed, I got it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and I've got plenty of (PLSS) oxygen so we're in good shape for an Auto Repress.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Come on, baby, there it comes.
(Repress becomes audible) You can go to
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, just the one regulator your using.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Your next move will be to get your PLSS O2, Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, get your PLSS O2, Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think that I've got it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: "Verify cabin pressure stable at 4.6 to 5.0." We'll watch it here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You don't need your (garbled) Depress (garbled) but you don't need it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Verify your circuit breakers...White Dots out plus EVA decals.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stay at (circuit breaker panel) 16 now, ECS Suit Fan 2, Closed?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Caution lights are on, that's good.
Until the Sep(arator)...Wait, ECS Caution...They'll go out when it (the Separator) winds up.
Doff gloves, stow on comm panel.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Going to have to put them on again, in a few minutes.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I have never seen so much dirt and dust in my whole life.
(Pause) Ron's not going to be able to see through either one of these helmet visors.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: But they sure do get scratched, if you're not careful.
(Long Pause) I think it's harder getting them (the gloves) off, these days, than it is getting them on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're right there (on the cue card).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We want to take a double look at something down there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, "disconnect OPS hose." (Long Pause) Oh boy!
That lock/lock is just tight on there, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: "Connect LM hoses, red to red, and blue to blue." We've got to do that this time, because we've got to dump the PLSSs.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me get out of your way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I'm out of the way now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How would you like to get off the PLSS water and get some spacecraft water, too?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You might unhook that stuff up there so you can get to your hoses.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can get it, I think.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Okay, we want red to red and blue to blue.
(Pause) We got to verify these, too, because...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You bet, I wouldn't leave for the world.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, I'm hooked up and locked.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And I'll do it for you, if you want.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, see if you can't find this one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me take a look at yours.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes, sir; Suit Flow on both of them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: PLSS pump Off and PLSS fan Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Boy, I never thought air could feel so cool.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, the PLSS water is disconnected.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How about pushing on that (spacecraft water hose to seat it in the connector)?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, but I want to see it first.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You don't have your visor down.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, we're both going off the air.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, zap me with it, and go "O", and then put your audio breaker, Open; and connect the LM comm.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Next thing, VHF B...Wait a minute.
You get the audio breaker, Open...Closed...Okay...VHF squelch B LMP.
Okay, noise threshold, plus one and a half.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: VHF A, Receive, and B, Off.
(Pause) A, Receive and B is Off here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We copy you loud and clear on LM comm.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Jack and Gene, we have a couple of - in fact, we have three - records here to read out to you guys.
One, the longest single EVA, 7 hours 37 minutes and 22 seconds.
The longest total lunar surface EVA time 22 hours 5 minutes and 6 seconds.
And a summary: the total lunar surface EVA time for the Apollo Program, 80 hours 44 minutes and 8 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That's quite a tribute to the people who made it possible.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I can't speak as authoritatively as some people have tonight, but for all of us around me, I'll say thank you, also.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Hey, Gene and Ron, this is the CSM CapCom.
Your buddy (Ron Evans) up above you there is chugging on and about ready to bed down himself, right now.

And he did take a good look at the landing site through binoculars tonight and took a good look at Shorty Crater there, and plotted out some variations in color that may be the same color changes you saw - that orange soil and that.
We're trying to match it all up.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Tell him we'll see him tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How's (Command Module) America looking to you, Bob?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: No problems at all and we got good SIM bay data on everything.
The UV, the IR, the Lunar Sounder.
And every data point we can see is just great.
It's just hardly any anomalies at all.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Gene, about the total limit of any problem up there is - and it's not a problem - is we're just having to stir those H2 tanks manually because of that limiting cycle on the pressure switch there.
We could go back to Auto but it's easier to go Manual.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: I'll be back up there tomorrow and I'll stir them for you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And Jack and Gene, let me make a note here for you guys.

There will be a series of references to this throughout the checklist; but there's a general thing and you might even put a piece of tape across it if you want to or something - rather than go through (the checklist) and call out (changes at) all the locations.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Bob, I think we'll handle that one okay.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene, there's one thing you may be interested in as the Commander.
We're going to have to do two burns tomorrow on America.

The mascons didn't deteriorate the orbit as much as everybody thought it was, so there's going to be an RCS (Reaction Control System) burn about an hour prior to the LOPC (Lunar Orbit Plane Change) burn.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Are you going to do a DOI 3, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, yeah, I guess that's what it'll be.
It's going to be an RCS burn at about 11 foot per second.
It'll drop the...It'll circularize the orbit and then we'll do the plane change burn.
transcript.speaker: Overmyer

tokens: Okay, and 17, we'd like you to press on reasonably diligently tonight.
You're just about on schedule but if we can turn off this Marine (Overmyer), we'd like you guys to press on.
We're looking at a nominal launch time and we've used up, of course, all the MCC (Mission Control Center)-H conference but we think you're within a few minutes of being right on.
If you can press on like you did last night we'll be in great shape.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I never stopped doing what I wanted to do anyway even though a Marine was talking.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Houston this is the LMP.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That might be ...I started...Let me bleed it off and let me see what it regulates at next time.
I didn't have my hose locked, Bob, and it came off the first try.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, we'll take another OPS check later on when we stow them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, they're saying we better do that (second check) before you throw out the PLSSs because we have to verify a good one before you dump the PLSSs.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Houston, we rechecked the LMP's OPS and it's regulating at 4.25, again.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, it's done that twice now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and it's steady once you do a check, right?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We've been watching it for about a minute, now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, we'll go with it then, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Bob, we're going to start the (sample) weighing process here...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...It might take a couple minutes to get things squared away.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're ready to copy the weights.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, sample (bag) 15 Echo has a bunch of dust that gradually accumulated in my (shin) pocket.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You can't go collecting samples after the EVA's over!
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Say Bob, right now I can't find the sample containment bag number 5.
Number 5 collection bag will be in (containment) bag 3.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and we're going to cross out "3" on the bag, and put a "5" on it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, or I think we could keep track of it otherwise; but that's fine.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, bag 7 is 32 (pounds), bag 4 is 31.5, bag 5 is 21, the big bag is 71, the ISA is 22.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, we have those five weights there, Geno.
We have 32 for number 7, 31.5 for number 4, 21 for number 5, 71 for the big bag, and 22 for the ISA.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That's affirm, and we're standing by for your Go for jettison.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Challenger, we are ready for jettison.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And Challenger, we'd like to keep out the original BSLSS bag, the one that you launched with.
We think we're going to need that to stow samples in.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Or, it's in (the spacecraft), we hope.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Gene) I've got it over here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We want it (the BSLSS bag) kept in the cabin, right.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, the recorder's not giving us any recording though.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...see if I can't get a little grease, yet, out of some of these things.
(Pause) Don your gloves, Jack, if you're...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't think I left it on.
(Pause) I probably did, if it's on now.
(Pause) I thought I read it in the checklist though.
(Pause) Don Arabian will never forgive me!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Shall we save ourselves some ascent weight?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well it was okay up until...It was just this EVA, if it was on, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Because it was working when we prepped, I'm sure of that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You can don your EV gloves.
And we'll check each others connectors again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll take my cuff checklists off.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Needless to say, you don't have to put your dust covers on, Jack.
If that makes you feel better.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, how long were we out today, 7 what?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stand by, we got it here someplace.
7 hours (pause while somebody in Houston finds the number) 7 hours 15 minutes and 31 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: How many kilometers did we put on the Rover?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We have an approximate total of about 36.1.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Boy, this one (glove) is really getting stiff.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Probably another 1/2 kilometer on that (total) when the Nav wasn't working.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah; since we didn't get distance readouts all the time, we sort of interpolated those distances there, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You don't have to push on the button to close it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I (garbled) this thing down there so it doesn't...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you want to check mine?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, "Suit circuits shall not be maintained at elevated pressure greater than 5 minutes".
Okay, we want to do an integrity check here.
And we're not going to use Reg(ulator) A at all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now you can verify all that other stuff.
Can you get it back there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Wait a minute (garbled), I should have (garbled).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How high do they want the suit?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Yeah, you can give me some water.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Off the peg (on the pressure gauge).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You did go Egress (on Press Reg B), right?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Laughs) (Pause) Well, it's supposed to be possible to do.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, we've got another 15 seconds to go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And 17, we're watching you, and you look good to us.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, I had about two tenths (psi pressure decay).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, let's make sure we got everything.
You went to 4.0, then you went to Egress then we monitor.
Suit Circuit Relief, Auto; pressure is decaying at 4.8.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, (on circuit breaker panel) 16 ECS Cabin Repress, Open.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This time I think I'll get this valve down here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They want this left in Auto?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Get that one down there, now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll give you a call at 3.5.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I want you (to go) Auto at 3.5, I give you a call my suit is going up.
"Verify cabin 3.5 and LM suit circuit locked up at 4.3 and decaying." Okay, it's about 4.6 and decaying.
(Pause) How's it look to you, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Overhead...(Correcting himself) Make it the forward dump, Open.
And I'll verify we lock up (garbled).
(Pause) Locking up, and the cabin's at 1.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, when I get the hatch partially opened, you can go to Auto on that valve.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Still no good words about the gravimeter (the LSG), huh, Bob?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, there's an outside chance that it's been a little cold.
And they're hoping that if it warms up, that it may take care of itself; but, no, everybody's very sad about that.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, I could have sprinkled dirt on it, maybe.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me go after the hatch, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Okay, you want that in Auto?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Boy I wish it'd take some of that dust out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And Danny's not out there to hand us in the lightweight PLSSs!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Here goes the old...Whose PLSS is this now?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, look at it, if you want a memory.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That must be yours; it's red.
Here goes the old Commander's PLSS.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, baby thanks for doing a good job.
And that was a backup PLSS too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It went down as gracefully as you did.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I can give you some of these.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, Houston, I think we ought to probably just mention, anyway.
We are jettisoning two sets of EVA gloves.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just like everything else did its job.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ooops, we didn't get them clear.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, we copy two sets of EVA gloves to the surface for the last time.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Laughs) What else have you got there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Garbled) that OPS stays where it is.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Okay, get it down there and then put your foot against it and it'll probably go.
(Pause) The only geologist's PLSS on the Moon.
(Pause) (To Gene, who is kicking the PLSS out the hatch) Good boy.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Too bad we don't have a broom (to sweep the dust out).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Get to do this again tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me see if I can't lock it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They all are auto and locked.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: "Master Alarm and Cabin Warning Light (should come) on." There it is.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Okay, Houston, Challenger; we're going to take off our gloves.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Okay, you're Go to unsuit there, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, and we can get our helmets off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If I can ever get unsuited.
Let me try the other one.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, let me get it for you.
No sense...Yeah, but I'm...(Both laugh) There.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The right one went easy last night.
I think they're all really getting (dusty)...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, and the helmet is off, and I'm stowing it in the BRA!
(Pause) Well, there's no changing our minds now (about a fourth EVA), the PLSSs are going to be hard to retrieve.
(Pause) But you could if you had to, though.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: From the old backup crew that followed you every step of the way, super job on EVA you guys.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Thinking he's talking to John Young) Thank you, John.
But we also appreciate your helping us get it this far.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, you know, and all those things you tell people...(Hearing Charlie's "Neil") Was that Charlie!?

I haven't heard your voice since down...You know all those good things you tell us about dust and all those other things: you know, you believe them all just like everybody else does, but you've just got to come out here and experience it for yourself to really be a believer.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, well I take it back about it all looks the same.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, it really doesn't, Charlie; but all those physical things you get handicapped with...There's a lot of easy things as far as one-sixth g, but all those other things (you've got to experience).
You know there's nothing like doing it to be a believer.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, you guys did it great.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, it may all look the same, but Taurus-Littrow, mark my words, has some variety.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, we could tell that, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Thank you, Charlie; and thank you for all the help.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Charlie, remember a long time ago when I said something about being down among them.
I didn't know what it was until we got here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Challenger, we have a good word from the old program manager...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Jack) Where are we (in the checklist)?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...even though you guys were pretty piggy there in bringing rocks back, we're going to let you keep them all.
You only busted the red line (that is, are overweight) by 40 pounds.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: He's a pretty good guy any way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That assumes your good buddy upstairs gets a good plane change tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Oh, he will; and I'll tell you, Gene and I both have lost 20 pounds apiece on this mission.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, specially when you don't know you're talking.
(Pause) Okay, we came to the end of the EVA-3 prep and post card.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're following you to the surface checklist.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Hey, Jack and I are going to frame this page
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm going to take the front half.
Gene will take the back half.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Roberto, we're going to manage the old batteries.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and Challenger we're ready to manage the old batteries.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Pause) (To Gene) I was just going to say I wish we had a broom.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, we're happy with your battery management.
We're ready for you guys to go to Low (data transmission rate).
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Sounding stuffy and/or tired) And, Challenger, it's Bob.
I'm going to turn you over to Casper about now, and let him put you guys to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, I'm not sure what you mean.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: He doesn't know anything about the LM.
He doesn't know anything about the LM.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's never too late to learn.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: For you, I'd believe that; for a lot of people, I wouldn't.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You guys make a pretty interesting show to watch.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: (Long Pause; Jack can hear Mattingly and others in the Control Room) All I can do is hear your breathing, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You can tell your friends off to the left there (at the Surgeon's Console) that I've turned the biomed off.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm sitting here looking at a couple of questions that they wanted to ask.
I'm not sure just how busy you are right now; and just keep in mind I've got a few questions to ask you on the traverses, and give me a call when you're ready to talk about it.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Let us get unsuited, and then we'll be back with you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Just whenever it's convenient for you.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: How do you read Challenger CDR?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How would you feel about this (Lithium Hydroxide) canister being changed now?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: For your information, we're trying to negotiate some time for you here.
We're about one and a half (hours) down.
We know how to pick up one hour of it, but we haven't quite figured out how to gain the other half (hour).
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Well, we're all unsuited now, and we're about ready to talk and eat at the same time, or listen and eat here at the same time.
And it won't be long and we'll be ready to hit the sack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Ken's trying to negotiate the question sessions for you here.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Okay, Ken, we're on and ready for that (fifteen minute EVA) debriefing.
And you should be getting Jack's biomed also.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Before we start on those questions, are you prepared to copy some lift-off times in your data book, and that kind of stuff?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Give us about 10 seconds (to get the Data Card book out).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay; I'll give you just the lift-off times.
For rev 45: 176 plus 12 plus 19; 178 plus 10 plus 49; 180 plus 09 plus 20; 182 plus 07 plus 50; 184 plus 06 plus 20.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: And what's the present rev, please?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; we're coming up on...In fact, it looks like we're in 44 right now.
(Pause) And, Jack, how about let's confirm that the rev 49 was 184:06:20.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Oh, I did have that wrong, in the seconds.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; and we've got your biomed coming through.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Well, one...(Hearing Mattingly) Am I alive?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ken, I'd like to believe that you read that one wrong, because I've gone through 50 copies on that without a mistake (during training).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I'll settle that with you when you get back.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: ...looks like it's about time for an eat period, and I've got these questions for you.
But let's keep in mind that that's secondary, and if it ever gets in the way of eating, why holler up, and we'll just drop it right there.
We're about an hour and a half behind the timeline, and we're going to make up no more than an hour of that.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We're cutting into the chow and go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Would you like for me to just read you all the questions, and let you mull those over before you work on it, or you want to do one at a time?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: One at a time's better, Ken.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Wanted to know if the blue-gray rocks at Station 6 are similar to those at Station 2?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: But I think you'll find that the ones at Station 6 are (a) much more metamorphic rock, or recrystallized rock, than the ones we had at Station 2.

(Pause) I had the impression that the ones we were sampling at Station 6 were really inclusions in the anorthositic gabbro and had been probably considerably metamorphosed by it being included in it; whereas, the ones we had at Station 2 were a separate rock type apparently, as I recall it, anyway.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ken, let me just say that my impression is that there was a lot more action in the rocks at Station 6 than 2.
I saw a lot more...A lot more was evident, (such as) the inclusions and some of the patterns (and) some of the other things we saw.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's go on to the second one, and it said: "Do we understand that there were no breccias at Station 8?" (Pause)
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Ken, that parent orthopyroxene plagioclase rock was a breccia in the sense (that) it was fractured and was injected by dark glass.
But it would be what we would call a mosaic breccia, in that respect, I think, and we didn't see any Station 6- or Station 2-type breccias there at all.
Other than the subfloor gabbro, that orthopyroxene plagioclase rock was the only major rock type I think we saw, unless we picked up some in the rake sample.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Okay; the third one says: "What are your impressions of the distribution of the familiar subfloor gabbros throughout the EVA-3 traverse?" (Pause)
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Well, I think we discussed that a little bit on the traverse - quite a bit, as a matter of fact.

The impression I had was that (during) most of the traverse on the plains - with the one exception of Van Serg Crater - we were in block fields or fragment fields that were almost...well, (that) were dominantly subfloor.
And, visually from the Rover, I had no impression of any other significant rock type, with the exception of occasional blocks of the gray variety of the subfloor gabbro.
I don't know what Gene's impression was.
He was driving a lot, but...Pass it on.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think we actually even commented - when we hit the break in slope coming back out of Station 6 and 7, and then coming back down out of 8 - how the terrain features changed.
I think that was due principally to what we've been calling the subfloor material (being) evident.

And there again, it was, what I would say, particularly mantled, filleted, much like we have here where the LM is; with the exception of Van Serg, where we actually saw fragmental boulders for the most part - a lot less buried - sitting on the surface.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: "At Van Serg, some rocks were described as gray breccias, and some contained white fragments.
Was there a variety of breccias present?"
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: My impression was that there was a variety only in the degree to which they were fractured.

We found and sampled, I think, the two major...One (was a) extremely fractured rock that I said was friable; anyway, it broke into small pieces very easily with a hammer or in your hand, if you worked at it.
And the other was a breccia that was much more cohesive than that.
It was not fractured or friable at all, but they both were on the rim, and I think they were just varieties, probably, of shock fracturing.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: "Could the Van Serg breccias correlate with the blue-gray material at Cochise?" (Pause)
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: But my first guess would be that the blue-gray at Cochise was blue-gray subfloor.
And...(Thinking it over) Well, I don't know.
Maybe with the pictures we have, we can work out an approximate attitude on that contact that I talked about in Cochise, and see if it would project over reasonably to Van Serg.
I wouldn't be surprised if it would.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: To me they looked very similar.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And you guys sure you're eating?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We're fixing and eating at the same time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You were starting to say something.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I think (that), from the distance (from which) we saw the blue-gray in Cochise, you couldn't make a definite correlation.
But it's a good idea and ought to be considered as one of the possibilities.

The other is that we just had a window in the subfloor that coincidentally - I mean (that) one (unit) underneath the subfloor might be that breccia - and, coincidentally, the Van Serg impact hit that window.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: "Can you tell us anything about the cowpie at Van Serg?
Was it a clast in the breccia?"
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: It was a...Excuse me; I have my mouth full.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It was an aggregate of irregular - looked like - agglutinated glass in (broken) fragments just sitting on the rim of Van Serg.
And the reason I said I thought it was "in place" or had fallen there and crystallized (that is, cooled) there, is that there were four or five similar fragments arranged in a small coherent area.
(I'm) not making that very clear I don't think; but it looks as if it hit and broke apart upon hitting a little bit but didn't really splatter or break apart in any significant manner.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There are similar things...I tell you what it looks like.

If anybody's walked up the rim of Kilauea Iki (on the island of Hawaii) - in the ash out there - and on top of the ash, there are bombs that were fairly clearly molten when they hit, and they had just enough strength to break when they hit.
But the individual pieces didn't move very far at all.
And you can see that pattern on Kilauea Iki.
And it was the same kind of thing, except that there was no directional aspect of it here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And that's not to say it's volcanic glass.
That's just the kind of pattern it was.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: "Can you tell us if the darker material in the bottom of Van Serg was similar to the collected rim material?"
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I think so, except - as Gene pointed out - the clasts were coarser.
They were coarser in the bottom than about anything we saw in the rim.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: "Are there any distinctive features, other than color, to separate tan from blue-gray breccias, such as jointing, or massive nature, continuity, anything of that nature?" (Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Yeah, we're...(To Jack) Where did we find those tan breccias?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think we were talking about some of them, I think, at Station 1 the first night.
In fact, didn't we have two of those in the same rock together?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, they were tan gabbros and blue-gray gabbros.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, yesterday...Excuse me, I wasn't reading the question.
There were tan and blue-gray breccias yesterday at Station 2, were there not?
You had the two types of breccias at Station 2.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And, now as I think back, I guess that's the main difference between the tan rocks at Station 2 and Station 6; because the ones at 6 appear to have an igneous texture or at least a very crystalline texture and inclusion-like masses of other rocks.
Whereas, the ones at Station 2, they seem to be fragment breccias, as I recall.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Although they may have been recrystallized or metamorphosed, they were clearly breccias at Station 2.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and "can you amplify your description going out to Station 6?
In particular were there blue-gray and tan-gray bands on the North Massif?" (Pause)
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Rather than bands, there were lines that appeared to be the upper terminus of the source of the boulders that were strewn below that line.
And those lines tended to be either...(to) show a blue-gray source or a tan-gray source, if you will.
(Garbled for a few seconds) Oh, those (garbled) bags.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Challenger, if you think you're talking to us, you're breaking up badly.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I just thought you might be interested; we just had a little spurt of dust come up by the window.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Was there a sleigh with it?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Did you see very much of dust, or was it just one little shot?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: One little shot, it was actually just particles.
Something we threw out must have popped.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: "Do you have any preliminary stratigraphic sequence for the plains?"
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: (Pause) Well, my guess would be that the Van Serg breccias were the oldest rocks.
The subfloor gabbro's the next oldest; and the mantling material's the youngest.
But the only good clear relationship was mantle on top of the subfloor gabbros.

We really don't have a good relationship of the (Van Serg) breccias and I guess I lean towards thinking that Van Serg was a window in the subfloor rather than being a bed of some kind on top of the subfloor.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and "do you have an opinion on what underlies the Sculptured Hills?"
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Well, I think, we said...The rake sample is probably going to have to tell the tale there.
My guess is - from the boulders of subfloor around up there that are of gabbro - that maybe the Sculptured Hills are a version of the subfloor rocks.
I don't think that the orthopyroxene anorthosite rock was necessarily indigenous to the Sculptured Hills.
It was glass-coated and permeated by glass, so I suspect it may have been thrown there by an impact somewhere else.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: All right sir, we've got one last thing for you to clean up.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, understand Auto and you got the Select to Cabin?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Yeah, Gas Diverter's pushed to Cabin and we're drying the PGAs (the suits) out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I guess if you could go in...My feeling is if you go to the bottom of every one of those large craters like Camelot, and you'd examine some of these fragments on the walls and down into the bottom, I just get a feeling you'd find this blue-gray breccia down there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I mean in all the big craters like Camelot.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think maybe that's true; however, we do not see isolated fragments of it very often, if at all, out here on the plains themselves, away from the craters.

So if the blue-gray breccia - (correcting himself) the Van Serg breccia - does underlie the subfloor, it's far enough down (below the surface) that the craters we have apparently have not penetrated and brought up much of that kind of material.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay guys, it's time to press on and finish up chow time and I've got your stowage summaries whenever you're ready for that, to get started on.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, Ken let us finish eating, then we'll go back to work.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, give me a call when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Ken I'd like to go ahead and hear your recommendations on stowage and I'll write it down.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On (Surface Checklist) page 2-2, I've got some numbers to fill in at the bottom under the collection bag stowage.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, number 1 with the aft of the engine cover is bag 8.
And then the second line, it's bag 7.
The third line it's bag 6 left-hand and 5 right-hand.
And the last line is bag 2 and 4.
And you can disregard the max.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, just so we got it straight: aft engine cover, bag 8; left hand side, bag 7.
Left hand plus the right-hand side: number 6 left-hand and 5 right.
And the ISA (Interim Stowage Assembly) bags 2 and 4.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Jack, the people down here watching things noticed that your
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I'm glad somebody is watching things!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ken we're in the process of getting all these bags in the proper places, now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Why don't you make a note that mag Bravo (Apollo magazine 134) is empty, with miscellaneous photos since the last report on it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, Ken, we're stowing mag Nancy (Apollo magazine 143) at a reading (a frame count of) 153.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: All we've got left to stow now is the Buddy SLSS bag and that's in work.
And we got all the ETB stuff taken care of.
All the other bags are stowed per your recommendation.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Geno, the guys are looking at that Buddy SLSS bag and suggested you all make sure that you're going to have room to do the equipment jettison and get the hatch open and all that.
They had planned on stowing it the next day.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The fact is that probably makes things more convenient.
We were going to be smart and get ahead here but, thank you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: "The faster I run, the behinder I get." (Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: You keep forgetting these checklists have been exercised a hundred thousand times.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you'll just give us a call when you're ready to sack out, we won't bother you.
That way we can keep track of what you're doing and when you're about ready to go to bed and we'll get you up at an appropriate time.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I guess I have the "duty biomed" tonight, so I'll give you a call when we're turning out the lights.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And it won't be too long.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You guys are doing pretty good!
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Hey, Ken, working through this thing, we haven't been able to find instructions for the stowage of the EV gloves.
Do your friends back there have any recommendations?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, stand by one and I'll check on that.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, Jack, we can stick those things on the comm panel for now, and then tomorrow on page
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: They're going to get the inside of the
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, from what we've seen, Jack, (chuckling) I think everything's going to be about the same color by the time you get through.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, it didn't bother your (Apollo 16) EVA - did it, Ken - to have a little dust in your helmet?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, no; that's kind of nice to have.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Hey, an experienced fellow like you might have a recommendation on how to get my visor up.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah; give it to the CMP.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We were just debating down here how come you guys threw away those nice clean gloves and kept the dirty ones.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: I wish you hadn't asked that, Deke.
(Slayton laughs) We were just debating that, too.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How long are your arms, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: You do all sorts of things...(Stops to listen to Slayton)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, they're out on the porch as a matter of fact.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess there's some old friends you just hate to get rid of, Deke.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Long Pause) Yeah, you guys have had some real winners there.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Well, they seemed to do all right for us.
I guess that's the way we were halfway thinking, but not thinking very well, as is witnessed in our checklist procedures tonight.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: On your comm, your checklist will call for going to Down-voice Backup.
And, tonight, we'd rather just leave it in the Normal Voice.
So, if you leave the configuration you have, rather than change it, that would be a good deal for us.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, Ken, I only got part of that, I was scrubbing my face here.
You want to save the same comm configuration we've got right now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And I'll be off comm here just for a few minutes - (correcting himself) (off) biomed - but I'll be back with you when I turn in.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I may have misled you earlier when I said, we weren't going to lose any time.
We can get you 8 hours (of rest) up to about 19 after the hour.
After that, we're going to have to start rearranging things to get 8 hours.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I just...I didn't want to mislead you.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: That's all right, you've never misled me before.
(Pause) Well, let me think about that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I was going to say: you catch on awful slow, if that's true.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Ken, I'm going to take off my headset here and jump into the hammock.
What time (are) we getting up Central Time?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, it's going to be roughly 45 minutes past the time listed at 182:39.
So...Are you asking for it in local time?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: My watch is set on Houston time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It won't...We're just cleaning up a few minor things and we'll actually probably be asleep in the next 10 to 15 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Sorry to keep...(Stops to listen) Thank you, boss.
Sorry to keep you up so late.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Gene, before you unplug you might...
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, I'm going off the air.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Looks like it's not flowing, if that's the configuration you want.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Yeah, we've got them both flowing.
And we've got good circulation in the cockpit.
If it looks good to you down there, we're in good shape up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan
